title: Beeswax pillar candle
subtitle: A poured beeswax pillar, naturally honey-scented
excerpt: A poured beeswax pillar candle in a standard 50 mm diameter mould. Beeswax is naturally scented with honey and propolis; no added fragrance is needed. The candle burns slowly with a warm golden flame and produces very little soot.
sourceNotes: CandleScience beeswax notes (candlescience.com, accessed May 2026), pour temperature and wick sizing for poured beeswax pillars. Bramble Berry pillar candle notes (brambleberry.com, accessed May 2026), shrinkage and second-pour technique.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 × 50 mm diameter pillar candle (~20-25 burn hours)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale to fill multiple pillar moulds; beeswax is expensive so batching in sets of 2-4 is the practical limit.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Allow 24 hours of cure before burning for the wax to fully set. One week gives a better burn.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Effectively indefinite. Beeswax does not go off. The honey scent fades over years but the candle remains perfectly usable. Store wrapped in paper to prevent dust accumulation on the surface.

glossaryTerms.definition: The temperature at which a wax becomes fully liquid. Beeswax melts at 62-65°C; it is poured 10-15°C above its melt point to allow time to fill the mould before it starts to set.

The first burn of a pillar candle sets the pattern; a burn stopped too early before the melt pool nears the edge will leave a permanent wax wall. Pillar candles are different from container candles: the goal is a melt pool 1-2 cm from the edge, not all the way to it.

content.text: A poured beeswax pillar candle. Beeswax is the highest-quality natural candle wax: a higher melt point than soy or paraffin (62-65°C), a very slow, clean burn, a warm golden colour, and a natural honey-and-propolis scent. No added fragrance is needed. The candle burns in a tall narrow melt pool, leaving the outer walls intact; this is the characteristic burn of a well-wicked beeswax pillar.

Beeswax shrinks significantly as it cools in the mould, typically leaving a sinkhole around the wick within 2 hours of pouring. A second top-up pour at 70°C fills the sinkhole; this is standard practice with beeswax pillars, not a sign that the first pour went wrong.
Melt wax on a simmering-water bath, not directly on the hob. Never leave melting wax unattended. Keep a kitchen fire blanket within reach.
What you need

items.prepNote: yellow beeswax pellets or a block cut into small pieces. A 50 mm diameter × 10 cm tall pillar mould takes approximately 180-200 g.

square-braid cotton wick sized for a 50 mm diameter pillar. A 2/0 or 3/0 square braid is typical; check the wick supplier's sizing chart for beeswax specifically (beeswax needs a larger wick than soy for the same diameter).

content.text: Method
Thread and set the wick

Thread the wick through the wick hole at the base of the pillar mould. Pull it taut through the top, secure it over the wick-centring bar at the top of the mould, and seal the wick hole at the base with mould sealer or sticky putty. The wick should be centred, taut, and sealed before any wax is poured.
Melt the beeswax to 75-80°C
Place the beeswax in the pouring pitcher and set the pitcher in a saucepan of simmering water. Stir gently as the wax melts. Beeswax fully liquefies at 75-80°C; the
melt point
is 62-65°C but pour at the higher temperature to allow time to fill before it starts to cloud.
First pour at 75°C

When the beeswax reaches 75°C, pour steadily into the mould beside the wick, filling to within 2 cm of the top. The mould will feel warm immediately. Set aside and do not move for 2 hours. As the wax cools a sinkhole will form around the wick; this is normal contraction.
Second top-up pour at 70°C

Remelt the reserved wax (or a fresh small amount) to 70°C. Pierce the skin around the sinkhole with a skewer or cocktail stick to break the surface. Pour the top-up wax carefully into the sinkhole; it should flow in and fill the void. Do not overfill above the original surface. Leave for a further 24 hours until fully set before unmoulding.
Unmould

After 24 hours the candle should release cleanly from a polycarbonate or aluminium pillar mould. If the candle sticks, place the mould in the freezer for 15 minutes; the thermal contraction will release the candle. Trim the base wick flush with the base. Trim the top wick to 5 mm before burning.
First burn
Burn for 2-3 hours on the first burn, until a melt pool 1-2 cm from the outer edge forms. The
memory burn

for a pillar is different to a container candle; you want the outer wax wall to remain intact as a dam, not melt all the way through. If the melt pool threatens to reach the outer edge before 2 hours, extinguish and allow the wall to re-solidify.
Storage and shelf life

Effectively indefinite. Beeswax does not degrade. The natural honey scent fades over several years in storage but the candle remains perfectly usable. Store in a paper-lined box away from direct sunlight, which fades the colour. A light wax bloom (a whitish haze on the surface) is normal crystallisation and does not affect burn quality.
